revision.text:
{{Infobox royalty | name = Tachibana no Kachiko
{{lang|ja|橘嘉智子}} | image = Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG | consort = yes | succession = [[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | reign = August 21, 815 – June 5, 823 | succession1 = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | reign1 = April 23, 823 – March 2, 833  | succession2 = [[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | reign2 = March 26, 833 – June 17, 850 | birth_date = 786 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|850|6|17|786}} | death_place = Heian Kyō (Kyōto) | burial_date = | burial_place = | royal house = [[Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]] (by birth)
[[Imperial House of Japan]] (by marriage) | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] | issue = [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]]
[[Princess Seishi]]
Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[sn:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]]

Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]

(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[sn:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. {{lang|ja|橘嘉智子}} | image = Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG | consort = yes | succession = [[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | reign = August 21, 815 – June 5, 823 | succession1 = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | reign1 = April 23, 823 – March 2, 833  | succession2 = [[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | reign2 = March 26, 833 – June 17, 850 | birth_date = 786 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|850|6|17|786}} | death_place = Heian Kyō (Kyōto) | burial_date = | burial_place = | royal house = [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]] (by birth)
[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] (by marriage) | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] | issue = [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]]
[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]
Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[sn:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[sn:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]]

Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko

Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{wikidata link|Q1058216}}

[[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]][[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[ja:橘嘉智子]][[ko:다치바나노 가치코]][[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]][[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG|birth_date=0786|death_date={{death date|0850|6|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654}}|children={{ill|Hideyoshi-shinnō|WD=Q17224920}}; {{ill|Ninmyō|WD=Q349271}}; {{ill|Seishi-naishinnō|WD=Q7244822}}; {{ill|Yoshiko-naishinnō|WD=Q17210181}}|father={{ill|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|WD=Q11544317}}|religion={{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}}|native_name=橘嘉智子}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1058216]] Wikidata name: Tachibana no Kachiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Tachibana no Kachiko
{{lang|ja|橘嘉智子}} | image = Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG | consort = yes | succession = [[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | reign = August 21, 815 – June 5, 823 | succession1 = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | reign1 = April 23, 823 – March 2, 833  | succession2 = [[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | reign2 = March 26, 833 – June 17, 850 | birth_date = 786 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|850|6|17|786}} | death_place = Heian Kyō (Kyōto) | burial_date = | burial_place = | royal house = [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]] (by birth)
[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] (by marriage) | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] | issue = [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]]
[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]
Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{wikidata link|Q1058216}}

This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{wikidata link|Q1058216}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] (by marriage) | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] | issue = [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]]

Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. {{lang|ja|橘嘉智子}} | image = Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG | consort = yes | succession = [[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | reign = August 21, 815 – June 5, 823 | succession1 = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | reign1 = April 23, 823 – March 2, 833  | succession2 = [[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | reign2 = March 26, 833 – June 17, 850 | birth_date = 786 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|850|6|17|786}} | death_place = Heian Kyō (Kyōto) | burial_date = | burial_place = | royal house = [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]] (by birth)
[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] (by marriage) | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] | issue = [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]]
[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]
Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] }} [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{wikidata link|Q1058216}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] {{draft categories| }} [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{wikidata link|Q1058216}} [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{lang|ja|橘嘉智子}} | image = Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG | consort = yes | succession = [[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | reign = August 21, 815 – June 5, 823 | succession1 = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | reign1 = April 23, 823 – March 2, 833  | succession2 = [[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | reign2 = March 26, 833 – June 17, 850 | birth_date = 786 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|850|6|17|786}} | death_place = Heian Kyō (Kyōto) | burial_date = | burial_place = | royal house = [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]] (by birth)

Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]

[[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]][[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[ja:橘嘉智子]][[ko:다치바나노 가치코]][[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]][[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[zh:橘嘉智子]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]][[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[ja:橘嘉智子]][[ko:다치바나노 가치코]][[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]][[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[zh:橘嘉智子]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]][[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[ja:橘嘉智子]][[ko:다치바나노 가치코]][[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]][[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]][[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG|birth_date=0786|death_date={{death date|0850|6|17}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654}}|children={{ill|Hideyoshi-shinnō|WD=Q17224920}}; {{ill|Ninmyō|WD=Q349271}}; {{ill|Seishi-naishinnō|WD=Q7244822}}; {{ill|Yoshiko-naishinnō|WD=Q17210181}}|father={{ill|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|WD=Q11544317}}|religion={{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}}|native_name=橘嘉智子}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1058216]] Wikidata name: Tachibana no Kachiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Tachibana no Kachiko
{{lang|ja|橘嘉智子}} | image = Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin, Honolulu Museum of Art II.JPG | consort = yes | succession = [[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | reign = August 21, 815 – June 5, 823 | succession1 = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | reign1 = April 23, 823 – March 2, 833  | succession2 = [[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | reign2 = March 26, 833 – June 17, 850 | birth_date = 786 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|850|6|17|786}} | death_place = Heian Kyō (Kyōto) | burial_date = | burial_place = | royal house = [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana]] (by birth)
[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] (by marriage) | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] | issue = [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]]
[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]
Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]]. *Husband: [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later [[:en:Emperor Ninmyō|Emperor Ninmyō]] **Daughter: Imperial [[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]] (正子内親王; 810–879), married to [[:en:Emperor Junna|Emperor Junna]] **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''[[:en:Avalokitesvara (film)|Avalokitesvara]]'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, [[:en:Ryoko Nakano|Ryoko Nakano]] starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., [[:en:Edward Kamens|Edward Kamens]] and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]], 6th edition * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before=[[Fujiwara no Taishi (died 794)|Fujiwara no Taishi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress of Japan|Empress of Japan]] | after=Princess Koshi
(granted title posthumously)| years=815–823}} {{succession box | before=[[:en:Fujiwara no Ryoshi|Fujiwara no Ryoshi]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=823–833}} {{succession box | before=[[Takano no Niigasa]]
(granted title posthumously) | title=[[:en:Grand empress dowager of Japan|Grand empress dowager of Japan]] | after=[[:en:Princess Seishi|Princess Seishi]]| years=833–850}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Empress dowagers of Japan}} {{Grand empress dowagers of Japan}}]]]] [[en:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[fa:تاچیبانا نو کاچیکو]] [[fr:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[id:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[ja:橘嘉智子]] [[ko:다치바나노 가치코]] [[sv:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[th:ทาจิบานะ โนะ คาจิโกะ]] [[vi:Tachibana no Kachiko]] [[zh:橘嘉智子]] {{wikidata link|Q1058216}} [[Category:786 births]] [[Category:850 deaths]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:9th-century Buddhists]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became {{ill|Emperor Junna|en|Emperor Junna|WD=Q374136|lt=Emperor Junna}}. *Husband: {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga}} (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later {{ill|Emperor Ninmyō|en|Emperor Ninmyō|WD=Q349271|lt=Emperor Ninmyō}} **Daughter: Imperial {{ill|Princess Seishi|en|Princess Seishi|WD=Q7244822|lt=Princess Seishi}} (正子内親王; 810–879), married to {{ill|Emperor Junna|en|Emperor Junna|WD=Q374136|lt=Emperor Junna}} **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''{{ill|Avalokitesvara (film)|en|Avalokitesvara (film)|WD=Q16078584|lt=Avalokitesvara}}'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, {{ill|Ryoko Nakano|en|Ryoko Nakano|WD=Q1532064|lt=Ryoko Nakano}} starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., {{ill|Edward Kamens|en|Edward Kamens|WD=Q5343880|lt=Edward Kamens}} and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * {{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}, 6th edition * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. Princess Hideko
Prince Hidera
Princess Toshiko
Princess Yoshiko
Princess Shigeko | father = Tachibana no Kiyotomo | mother = Taguchi Michihime }} {{family name hatnote|Tachibana|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Tachibana no Kachiko'''|橘 嘉智子||786 – June 17, 850}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Empress Danrin'''|檀林皇后|Danrin-kōgō}}, was a Japanese empress, the chief consort of {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 318-319. and the daughter of {{nihongo|Tachibana no Kiyotomo|橘 清友}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 319. She was de facto ruler of the empire between 833 and 850. The empress was a devout Buddhist. She founded the Buddhist {{ill|Danrin-ji|en|Danrin-ji|WD=Q3015355|lt=Danrin-ji}} temple complex, and for this reason, she came to be called Danrin''-kōgō.'' She died in the 4th day of the 5th month of 850.Adolphson, Mikael ''et al.'' (2006). {{Google books|UjB8yiiGDykC|''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries,'' p. 23.|page=23}} Known for her renowned beauty in her life, on her deathbed, Empress Danrin requested her body to be left open to the environment for the public to see the effects of human decomposition. This event later became a popular Japanese folk legend and was later depicted by the 18th century painting "[https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:kk]][[Category:Nine_Stages_of_Decomposition_of_the_Heian_Period_Empress_Danrin Nine Stages of Decomposition of the Heian Period Empress Danrin]".{{cite web |title=The Empress Danrin was a beautiful woman and "Katabira no Tsuji," the entrance to her burial place |url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/en/makai/2019/08/katabiranotsuji/ |website=Leaf KYOTO |language=ja |date=1 June 2024}} ==Genealogy== Lady Kachiko was born to Tachibana no Kiyotomo and his wife, Taguchi Michihime. In June 809, Tachibana no Kachiko married the new emperor. The marriage produced seven children: two sons and five daughters. Her eldest son would succeed his father as Emperor of Japan and her eldest daughter married Prince Otomo, who later became {{ill|Emperor Junna|en|Emperor Junna|WD=Q374136|lt=Emperor Junna}}. *Husband: {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga}} (嵯峨天皇, Saga-tennō, October 3, 786 – August 24, 842) **Son: Imperial Prince Masara (正良親王) later {{ill|Emperor Ninmyō|en|Emperor Ninmyō|WD=Q349271|lt=Emperor Ninmyō}} **Daughter: Imperial {{ill|Princess Seishi|en|Princess Seishi|WD=Q7244822|lt=Princess Seishi}} (正子内親王; 810–879), married to {{ill|Emperor Junna|en|Emperor Junna|WD=Q374136|lt=Emperor Junna}} **Daughter: Imperial Princess Hideko (秀子内親王; d. 850) **Son: Imperial Prince Hidera (秀良親王; 817–895) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Toshiko (俊子内親王; d. 826) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Yoshiko (芳子内親王; d. 836) **Daughter: Imperial Princess Shigeko (繁子内親王; d. 865) ==In popular culture== In the acclaimed 2013 movie ''{{ill|Avalokitesvara (film)|en|Avalokitesvara (film)|WD=Q16078584|lt=Avalokitesvara}}'', a loose adaptation of the [[:en:Putuoshan|Putuoshan]] genesis story, {{ill|Ryoko Nakano|en|Ryoko Nakano|WD=Q1532064|lt=Ryoko Nakano}} starred as the Empress Dowager Tachibana Kachiko. ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Adolphson, Mikael S., {{ill|Edward Kamens|en|Edward Kamens|WD=Q5343880|lt=Edward Kamens}} and Stacie Matsumoto. (2006). [https://books.google.com/books?id=UjB8yiiGDykC&q=tachibana+no+kachiko ''Heian Japan, Centers and Peripheries.''] Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824830137}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/71542885 OCLC 71542885] * {{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}, 6th edition * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society.
